insights.insight: The industry pays $5–$150 CPA for modeled attribution. A cryptographically verified receipt settles at ~$0.10 and eliminates the attribution problem entirely — the receipt is the conversion event, not a proxy for it.

insights.topic: Cookie deprecation leaves a structural gap
insights.insight: With Chrome joining Safari and Firefox in blocking cross-site tracking, the ad stack's primary targeting primitive is gone. Device fingerprinting and data brokers are degrading under privacy law — no incumbent solution produces identity-free, cross-ecosystem purchase proof.

insights.topic: Platform silos are the vulnerability brands can exploit
insights.insight: Amazon sees Amazon purchases, Visa sees spend but not SKUs, Meta sees clicks but not conversions. A receipt-layer dataset sits below all platform silos and aggregates verified purchase signals none of them can produce individually.

insights.topic: Physical commerce receipts entering the dataset
insights.insight: Home Depot and Starbucks receipts appearing in consecutive snapshots signals a shift away from digital-only transaction data. Physical commerce receipts are harder to fabricate, carry higher per-transaction value, and are directly relevant to CPG and retail brand measurement.

insights.topic: Brand participation is not optional at equilibrium
insights.insight: Consumer-submitted receipts enter the dataset whether or not a brand has opted in — a Starbucks receipt is valid without Starbucks. Brands that stay out are still measured; they just lose the ability to shape or access the data about themselves.

A 17% receipt count drop and 13% GMV decline in a single snapshot (HB#92) marked the steepest recent contraction; acceleration has recovered to 1.001x but velocity remains at 0.597x. Brands evaluating this as a data source should monitor intake consistency before committing attribution budgets.
